record_office_terminology.land_records: Probate Judge (deeds, mortgages — in Alabama, the Probate Judge handles land records in most counties)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Probate Judge (marriage licenses; historically from territorial period)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1908 (births/deaths statewide registration began; spotty compliance until 1920s; earlier records at county and church level)

burned_county_authority: Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH), Montgomery — ADAH holds microfilm copies of many county records that may supplement or replace records lost in courthouse fires. Alabama has approximately 20 burned counties with significant record losses from courthouse fires, Civil War destruction, and other disasters. For burned counties, always check ADAH microfilm, FamilySearch digitized records, state-level duplicates (tax records, military records), and neighboring county records.
administrative_notes: Alabama became the 22nd state on December 14, 1819, carved from the Mississippi Territory. Originally inhabited by Creek, Cherokee, Chickasaw, and Choctaw nations who ceded lands through a series of treaties from 1805 to 1835. The state's 67 counties range from Tennessee River valley counties settled in the 1810s to Wiregrass counties formed after the Civil War. Alabama was a Confederate state (seceded January 11, 1861) and Montgomery served as the first Confederate capital. The Black Belt region (named for its dark, fertile soil) was one of the wealthiest cotton-producing areas in the antebellum South, with enslaved populations exceeding 80% in some counties. The Civil War caused significant record destruction in many counties. Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land from the federal government through the General Land Office. The Probate Judge is the primary county record officer, handling deeds, wills, marriages, and other vital records. The Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH) in Montgomery, established in 1901 as the first state-funded archives in the United States, is the central repository for state and county records. Vital records registration (births and deaths) did not begin statewide until 1908, and compliance was incomplete until the 1920s.

core_repositories.source_name: Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)
core_repositories.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History, Montgomery
core_repositories.description: The first state-funded archives in the United States (established 1901). ADAH is the primary repository for Alabama genealogical research, holding county records on microfilm (deeds, probate, marriages, tax records), state government records, military records (Confederate pensions, muster rolls), vital records, newspaper microfilm, manuscript collections, and photographs. The reference room in Montgomery provides access to microfilm readers and finding aids. ADAH also maintains an online catalog and selected digital collections.

core_repositories.source_name: Birmingham Public Library -- Department of Archives and Manuscripts
core_repositories.description: Major regional repository with extensive genealogical holdings for Jefferson County and north-central Alabama. Collections include city directories (Birmingham from 1883), photograph collections, manuscript collections, Tutwiler Collection of Southern History and Literature, and the Linn-Henley Research Library with family files, cemetery records, and church histories.

core_repositories.source_name: Huntsville-Madison County Public Library -- Heritage Room
core_repositories.description: Outstanding genealogical collection for the Tennessee Valley region. Holdings include family files, Bible records, cemetery transcriptions, newspaper clippings, manuscript collections, and microfilm. One of the best county-level genealogy collections in Alabama.

core_repositories.source_name: Mobile Public Library -- Local History and Genealogy Division
core_repositories.description: Primary genealogical collection for the Gulf Coast region. Holdings include colonial records (French, British, Spanish), family files, cemetery records, ship passenger lists, and Mobile city directories. Special collections on Mobile's Catholic heritage and Creole families.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Alabama county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, military records, and Freedmen's Bureau records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at ADAH and county courthouses. Key collections include Alabama marriages (1809-1950s), probate records, and county tax records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability charts, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Alabama's burned counties, record office terminology (Probate Judge), and research strategies for the state's unique challenges.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Alabama newspapers from the early 1800s. Major titles include the Mobile Register, Montgomery Advertiser, Huntsville Democrat, and county weeklies. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, marriage notices, and community news.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History, Montgomery
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: ADAH holds the most comprehensive collection of Alabama newspaper microfilm. Over 2,000 titles representing every county. Includes papers from the 1810s through the 20th century. Research room access at ADAH in Montgomery.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History, Montgomery
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: ADAH holds extensive records documenting enslaved persons in Alabama including county tax records listing enslaved persons by slaveholder, estate inventories naming enslaved individuals, bills of sale, manumission records, and plantation journals. The Black Belt counties (Dallas, Greene, Hale, Lowndes, Macon, Marengo, Perry, Pickens, Sumter, Wilcox) had the highest enslaved populations.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Freedmen's Bureau Records -- Alabama Field Offices
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (Freedmen's Bureau) for Alabama field offices (1865-1872). Includes labor contracts, marriage registrations, school records, land transactions, complaints, and ration records documenting formerly enslaved families during Reconstruction. FamilySearch has digitized and partially indexed these NARA records.

Archives of Tuskegee University (founded 1881 by Booker T. Washington). Holdings include Booker T. Washington papers, George Washington Carver papers, Tuskegee Airmen records (Moton Field), and extensive African American family and community records from Macon County and the Black Belt region.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Creek and Cherokee Removal Records -- NARA
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records documenting Creek and Cherokee removal from Alabama (1830s). Includes the Henderson Roll (1835, Cherokee census), Creek census rolls, removal emigration lists, claims files, and treaty records. Essential for tracing Native American families who inhabited Alabama before removal to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Creek War (1813-1814) military records also at NARA document both Creek and American participants.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Poarch Band of Creek Indians -- Tribal Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Poarch Band of Creek Indians, Atmore
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Poarch Band is the only federally recognized tribe in Alabama, located in Escambia and Baldwin counties. Tribal enrollment records and historical files document Creek families who remained in Alabama after removal. Essential for researchers with Poarch Creek ancestry.

military_records.source_name: Alabama Confederate Military Records -- ADAH / NARA
military_records.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History / NARA / FamilySearch
military_records.description: ADAH holds Confederate muster rolls, service records, and Confederate pension records for Alabama soldiers. Approximately 120,000 Alabamians served in the Confederate military. NARA compiled service records are indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry. Confederate pension applications at ADAH often provide biographical information, disability details, and widow/dependent statements.

military_records.source_name: Alabama Union Military Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
military_records.description: Service records for Alabama Unionists who served in Union forces, particularly the 1st Alabama Cavalry, USA and other Union Alabama regiments recruited from north Alabama hill counties (Winston, Walker, Fayette, Marion, Blount). Also includes USCT (United States Colored Troops) records for African American soldiers recruited in Alabama.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Alabama
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Alabama soldiers, both Confederate and Union. Alabama contributed approximately 120,000 Confederate soldiers and 3,000-5,000 Union soldiers.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records -- Alabama Federal Land Patents
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land directly from the federal government. BLM GLO website provides searchable federal land patents (cash entry, homestead, military bounty, etc.) for all Alabama counties. Includes survey plats and field notes. Essential for identifying first landowners in each county.

land_and_property.source_name: Alabama County Deed Records -- County Probate Judges / ADAH
land_and_property.repository: County Probate Judge offices / Alabama Department of Archives and History
land_and_property.description: Each of Alabama's 67 counties maintains deed records at the Probate Judge office. ADAH holds microfilm copies of historical deed books for most counties. Records date from county formation (1800 for Washington County through 1903 for Houston County). Burned counties have gaps; ADAH microfilm may fill some gaps with pre-fire copies.

vital_records.repository: Alabama Department of Public Health / Alabama Department of Archives and History
vital_records.description: Statewide birth and death registration began in 1908 but compliance was incomplete until the 1920s. The Alabama Department of Public Health (ADPH) Center for Health Statistics has records from 1908 to the present. ADAH has delayed birth certificates and some earlier vital records. For pre-1908 vital events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, newspaper notices, and county records (marriages from the Probate Judge, which predate 1908).

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Alabama Church Records -- ADAH / Local Congregations / FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for pre-1908 vital event research in Alabama. Baptist and Methodist churches dominate, with Presbyterian, Catholic (especially Mobile), and Episcopal congregations also present. ADAH has microfilmed some church records. Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Mobile, Catholic) has records from 1704. African American church records (Baptist, AME) are critical for post-Civil War family research.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Alabama. Growing coverage across all 67 counties. Alabama has many rural family cemeteries, church cemeteries, and plantation burial grounds. The Alabama Cemetery Database and USGenWeb Tombstone Project have additional transcriptions.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: Alabama Genealogical Society, Samford University, Birmingham
research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society affiliated with Samford University. Publishes The Alabama Genealogical Society Magazine. Maintains surname files, research guides, and promotes genealogical education. The Samford University Library has the AGS collection including published family histories and county records.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 67 Alabama counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, lookups, and research guides. Quality and coverage vary by county but provide free community-sourced data.

Regional genealogical society serving Lauderdale, Limestone, Madison, Morgan, Colbert, Lawrence, and Jackson counties in the Tennessee Valley region. Publishes Valley Leaves. Strong collection of family files and regional research resources.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Alabama has several extinct or renamed counties. Baine County (1866-1868) was renamed Etowah County. Baker County (1868-1874) was renamed Chilton County. Jones County (1867-1868) was renamed Sanford County, then Lamar County (1877). Cotaco County (1818-1821) was renamed Morgan County. Hancock County (1850-1858) was an early name for what became parts of Winston and Walker. Decatur County (1821-1825) was abolished and absorbed into Morgan County. Researchers should note that county name changes affect record searches — records filed under the original county name may not appear in searches under the current name. Additionally, Alabama Territory (1817-1819) records are at ADAH and NARA, and Mississippi Territory (1798-1817) records for the Alabama region are at the Mississippi Department of Archives and History and NARA.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Alabama has approximately 20 burned counties with significant record losses, primarily from courthouse fires and Civil War destruction. The most common causes were courthouse fires (often accidental) in the 1850s-1870s and deliberate destruction by Union forces during the Civil War. ADAH microfilm may contain copies made before the fires, and state-level records (tax rolls, military records, land patents) can supplement lost county records. The BLM General Land Office retains all federal land patent records regardless of county courthouse losses.
